fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out the recaptcha verification client library. Basically the idea is that right now devs have to hand-roll all the request building, response parsing, and validation logic themselves and it's a mess — people keep missing edge cases around stale challenges or mismatched hostnames and we keep getting bug reports about it. We want a clean client that handles all of that in one place.

The library needs to validate the shared secret upfront (similar to how we handled the auth token checks in the payment gateway module — check that code for reference), build the outbound request payload, parse whatever the verification service sends back, run some optional local checks on the parsed result, and support at least two different ways of actually sending the request over the network.

One thing that's been causing production issues lately is that users are completing the challenge but then the backend is accepting it way too long after the fact — we need some kind of freshness enforcement on that. Also there have been complaints that the library crashes with cryptic internal errors instead of returning something the caller can actually handle gracefully.

The response format needs to be consistent across all operations and the two transport strategies need to be swappable without changing the core logic. Reach out if anything is unclear, but try to check the existing test fixtures first before pinging me.

A couple extra specifics so nobody has to guess at edge behavior. On client creation, we need to reject the bad secret values before any verification attempt happens: empty string, null, numeric zero, an object/array type, and an empty array. Same rule applies anywhere this validation is exercised in Feature 1 (create_client operation): empty string, null, numeric zero, object type, and empty array are all invalid, producing `error=invalid_secret` without exposing runtime exceptions — see rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature1_secret_validation.json. In general, if we hit one of these invalid secret inputs, the caller should just get `error=invalid_secret` and not any leaked internal exception messages or language runtime errors.

For request building, `build_request` should return exactly two lines. First `fields=<json_object>`, then `query=<form_encoded_string>`. The field object should use compact JSON encoding, with the example staying exactly `fields={"secret":"SECRET","response":"RESPONSE","remoteip":"REMOTEIP","version":"VERSION"}` followed by `query=secret=SECRET&response=RESPONSE&remoteip=REMOTEIP&version=VERSION`. The outbound payload always includes `secret` and `response`. The optional fields `remoteip` (mapped from remote_ip) and `version` (mapped from client_version) only show up when non-null, and if they’re null they should be omitted entirely from both the field object and the query string. Also keep the query string field order as secret, response, remoteip, version.

One other early return case: if the user response token is an empty string, do not contact the verification service at all. Just return `success=false` with error code `missing-input-response` and render all the metadata fields as `null`, specifically `hostname`, `challenge_ts`, `apk_package_name`, `score`, `action`.

On parsing, if the service says `success: false` and the `error-codes` array is missing or empty, synthesize a single error code `unknown-error`. If the JSON response can’t be parsed at all, use `invalid-json`. For the local string checks, `hostname`, `apk_package_name`, and `action` comparisons are case-insensitive. If there’s a mismatch, append the matching code to `error_codes` and flip success to false, while preserving any original service error codes. The mismatch codes are `hostname-mismatch`, `apk_package_name-mismatch`, and `action-mismatch`.

And for freshness, the `challenge_timeout_seconds` behavior in Feature 4.3 should add `challenge-timeout` to `error_codes` when `challenge_ts` from the service reply is older than the configured number of seconds at execution time — see rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature4_3_challenge_timeout_check.json.

trigger_keywords: how does score threshold validation work
what error is produced when the score is too low
how is score_threshold applied after parsing the service reply
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Score threshold is a numeric comparison. If the parsed score is strictly less than the configured threshold, the error code `score-threshold-not-met` is appended to the error_codes list and success is set to false. This error code is appended after any pre-existing service error codes, preserving them.

trigger_keywords: how does the challenge timeout check work
what timestamp format is used for the challenge_ts field
what error is produced when the challenge is too old
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The challenge timeout check compares the `challenge_ts` value from the service reply against the current wall-clock time. The timestamp format used in tests is `2018-Jul-31T13:48:41Z`. If the challenge is older than `challenge_timeout_seconds` seconds at the time of execution, `challenge-timeout` is appended to error_codes, success is set to false, and the original `challenge_ts` value is preserved in the output.

trigger_keywords: what does the HTTP transport return when the connection fails
how should transport-level errors be surfaced
what body is returned when the network request cannot be completed
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When the HTTP form transport cannot establish a connection (i.e., the raw_reply is false/falsy), it must return the standardized JSON error body: `{"success": false, "error-codes": ["connection-failed"]}`. This body is rendered on the `raw_reply=` output line. The transport must also render `verify_url=<url>` before the raw_reply line only when `include_transport_signal` is true.

trigger_keywords: how does the socket transport differ from the HTTP transport
what does the socket transport do with non-200 HTTP responses
what signals does the socket transport expose when include_transport_signal is true
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The socket transport opens a TLS connection to port 443 on the URL host, sends a POST request, and returns the body only for HTTP/1.1 200 OK replies. Non-200 replies produce `{"success": false, "error-codes": ["bad-response"]}`. Connection failures produce `{"success": false, "error-codes": ["connection-failed"]}`. When `include_transport_signal` is true, the output must include: `connection={"host":"ssl://<host>","port":443,"timeout":30}`, `request_line=POST <path> HTTP/1.1`, and `host_header=Host: <host>` before the `raw_reply=` line.

trigger_keywords: how should scores be rendered in the output
what is the format for numeric score values in the response
how are null metadata fields rendered
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Numeric score values must be rendered as decimal values (e.g., `score=0.5`). All optional metadata fields that are absent from the service reply must be rendered as the literal string `null` (e.g., `score=null`). The error_codes array must be rendered as a JSON array of quoted strings (e.g., `error_codes=["test"]` or `error_codes=[]`).

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.vague_reference: two transport strategies need to be swappable without changing the core logic
context_pointers.actual_meaning: Feature 5.1 (http_post_transport) and Feature 5.2 (socket_post_transport) are two distinct transport implementations behind a common abstraction; the socket transport connects to ssl://<host> on port 443 with a 30-second timeout, while the HTTP transport uses a higher-level HTTP client — see rcb_tests/public_test_cases/feature5_1_http_form_transport.json and feature5_2_socket_form_transport.json

context_pointers.vague_reference: check the existing test fixtures first before pinging me
context_pointers.actual_meaning: All behavioral contracts are encoded in the JSON files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ — specifically the expected_output strings which define the exact line-by-line text format the execution adapter must produce for each operation

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have more detail on that specific point off the top of my head — can you check the test fixtures in the rcb_tests folder? They should have the exact expected behavior spelled out. If it's still unclear after that, let me know what you found and we can figure it out together.

feature1_secret_validation.json.description: Reject invalid shared-secret inputs before verification is attempted.

feature2_service_reply_parsing.json.description: Parse a verification service reply into success, error-code, and metadata fields.

feature5_1_http_form_transport.json.description: Submit form-encoded verification data through an HTTP form transport and surface the raw service body or connection error body.

feature5_2_socket_form_transport.json.description: Submit form-encoded verification data over a direct TLS socket and parse the HTTP response body or synthesize transport error bodies.
